The surgeon creates a small stab wound approximately five centimeters above the umbilicus for the insertion of a supraumbilical port, which will facilitate the laparoscopic myomectomy by providing more space for maneuvering and a panoramic view through the telescope.

The surgeon introduces the Veress needle while lifting the abdominal wall, initiating insufflation with the preset pressure set to fifteen mmHg. The actual pressure gradually increases, and the flow rate is maintained at one liter per minute.
The surgeon enlarges the incision and introduces the first optical trocar, an 11-millimeter trocar, into the abdominal cavity. The trocar is used for optics, and gas is attached for insufflation.

The surgeon performs an initial diagnostic laparoscopy, positioning two additional ports following the baseball diamond concept. The fibroid is palpated, measuring 18 centimeters, with the index finger and thumb used for spatial orientation relative to the umbilicus and snuff box.

The surgeon positions the telescope over the 24 cm arc and places the surgical instrument over the 18 cm arc according to the baseball diamond concept. Two ipsilateral and one contralateral ports are utilized for the procedure.

The surgeon prepares to insert a myoma screw to hold the fibroid securely, following the completion of vasopressin injection for hydro dissection.
The surgeon introduces the myoma screw with the left hand to stabilize the fibroid, while using a harmonic or monopolar device with the right hand to initiate the dissection plane around the fibroid.
The myoma screw is visibly pierced into the fibroid, and it is being used to lift the fibroid upward, providing better access for further dissection.
The surgeon identifies a sub-serous fibroid, which is not deeply embedded in the uterine muscle, and proceeds to make an annular-shaped incision around the fibroid.
The surgeon continues the annular-shaped incision around the sub-serous fibroid, maintaining the circular cut to facilitate its removal.
The surgeon creates an elliptical incision around the sub-serous fibroid, preparing to extend the dissection to either side.
The surgeon begins the dissection plane using a harmonic or monopolar device while the myoma screw stabilizes the fibroid.
The surgeon makes incisions from the left and right sides around the sub-serous fibroid, incorporating some of the serosa to facilitate easier removal.
Vasopressin is seen leaking from the surgical site following hydro dissection, forming a visible plane that facilitates the gradual extraction of the sub-serous fibroid from the uterine tissue.

The surgeon uses a specially designed, blunt metal rod for enucleation of the myoma, ensuring a stable and atraumatic dissection plane. The rod is complemented by the use of two myoma screws for enhanced manipulation.
The surgeon uses three laparoscopic ports to perform enucleation of the myoma. The myoma screw is removed and then reapplied over the base of the fibroid to secure it for further manipulation.

The fibroid is nearly extracted, and the surgeon rearranges the myoma screw to optimize force application. The screw is positioned in a favorable area, allowing continued separation of the fibroid from surrounding tissue.
The fibroid is gradually being extracted from the uterine tissue. The surgeon carefully avoids abrupt avulsion to prevent pulling on nearby vessels, especially as they approach the feeding vessel.

The fibroid is visible outside the uterine tissue, but some attachments of feeding vessels remain. The surgeon carefully uses energy sources to detach these vessels, avoiding abrupt avulsion to prevent bleeding. The base of the fibroid is examined, and remaining attachments are detached using a harmonic scalpel.

The surgeon carefully inspects the surgical field, identifying and separating the ureter laterally and a branch of the uterine artery. Using a ligature, the surgeon meticulously creates a dissection plane, ensuring gradual separation of the fibroid from surrounding tissues without causing avulsion.
The surgeon applies energy to manage bulging of the tube, posterior leaf of the broad ligament, or ureter, ensuring careful manipulation to prevent damage.

The surgeon carefully separates the tube from the surrounding tissue to avoid damage, revealing a large eighteen-centimeter fibroid. The uterus is exposed with a significant raw area, indicating the need for suturing.

The surgeon holds the round ligament and lifts it, while an assistant uses a port to stabilize the area. In cases with large intramural fibroids, a uterine manipulator is avoided to prevent potential perforation.
The surgeon mechanically lifts the fibroid, using instruments to elevate it within the surgical field.
The surgeon performs suction irrigation to remove clots and blood from the surgical area, clearing the field for further procedures.
The surgeon begins suturing the raw area and bed of the sub-serous fibroid, aiming to obliterate the exposed tissue surface.
The surgeon inserts a needle into the raw area of the uterus, visible as the needle penetrates the tissue.
A curved 30 mm needle is directly pushed through the wound, bypassing the ten ml cannula, as the endoske is not preferred for accessing posterior fibroids.
The surgeon begins suturing the lateral margins of the surgical site to approximate the tissue and prevent the ovary or fallopian tube from entering the space between the sutures.
The surgeon sutures the serosa layer to prevent the tube or ovary from entering the raw area created by the fibroid removal.
The surgeon ties a Dandy jamming knot to secure the sutures in place, ensuring the fibroid bed is properly closed.
The surgeon uses a pre-tied loop to secure the suture without knotting, demonstrating the automatic locking of the dandy jamming knot.
The surgeon begins continuous suturing of the raw area using a curved needle, repeatedly taking bites to approximate the tissue edges.
The surgeon performs side-to-side suturing, taking reverse bites on the posterior wall, which is visually more challenging than suturing the anterior wall.

The surgeon carefully handles the ovary and fallopian tube while taking reverse bites on the posterior uterine wall, ensuring the needle does not accidentally prick the tube. After a few initial bites, the suturing process becomes easier.
The surgeon holds the suture to maintain traction while continuing the suturing process in the cul-de-sac area, ensuring the entire area is sutured around the cervical fibroid.
The cul-de-sac area is visible, with the peritoneum completely stripped away, exposing the underlying fibroid. The rectum is located below this area.
The surgeon completes the suturing of the cul-de-sac area, ensuring all raw surfaces are covered with continuous stitches.
Any raw area should not be there.
The surgeon takes a straight bite with the needle at the lateral margin, preparing to suture it to the upper margin to obliterate the space in the cul-de-sac area.
The surgeon identifies the upper margin of the muscle and serosa, preparing to suture these layers together.
The surgeon continues the continuous suturing technique in the cul-de-sac area, ensuring complete closure of the tissue layers.
The surgeon completes the suturing of the area, ensuring that the tissue margins are properly aligned and secured.
The surgeon continues to perform continuous suturing along the upper margin of the muscle and serosa, ensuring that any raw areas are covered and the space is completely obliterated.

The surgeon approximates the interior margin of the muscle and serosa, pulling it into position. Multiple layers of suturing are performed to ensure proper closure, requiring careful and patient technique.
The surgeon carefully sutures the lateral edges of the posterior wall of the broad ligament, avoiding deep bites to prevent injury to the ureter or uterine artery.
The surgeon locks the suture to ensure the corners of the incision are approximated, maintaining tension and alignment of the tissue edges.
